A Developer Opened Cursor at 9 AM and Got Rate-Limited by Lunch. The Prompts Weren’t Even That Big.
Nothing about the session looked unusual. Reasonable prompts, no massive code dumps, and yet the budget was gone before noon. This is one of the most common complaints developers are searching for right now, and it traces back to one thing nobody explains when handing someone an AI coding tool: how token billing actually works.
Here’s the part most people never get told directly. Every single message you send re-sends the entire conversation history to the model. You’re not just paying for your new question, you’re paying to replay everything that came before it, every single time. A chat that’s grown to 80,000 tokens spends that much just catching the model up before it even reads what you actually asked.
It gets more counterintuitive. Output tokens cost five times more than input tokens. Thinking models bill their internal reasoning the same as visible output, which means asking a reasoning model to rename a variable can quietly trigger full reasoning effort unless you explicitly tell it not to. And deleting old messages from a chat doesn’t refund a single token already billed.
None of the fixes require a technical overhaul. They’re habit changes: knowing when to split a long chat, when a task actually needs a heavier model tier, and what to paste versus what to leave out.
This piece breaks down exactly how Claude and Cursor bill for tokens, the specific moments where credits quietly leak away, and a practical roadmap for keeping spend under control without slowing your work down.
Read the full guide here: Preserving AI Credits While Coding — A Practical Guide to Reducing Token Usage and Avoiding AI Rate Limits
